subject: AP US Government and Politics
theme: The three branches of government interact through a system of shared powers, checks and balances, and institutional competition that shapes policymaking and governance.
core_concepts: Congress is bicameral: the House (435 members, 2-year terms, proportional) controls revenue bills; the Senate (100 members, 6-year terms, equal) confirms appointments and ratifies treaties.
The legislative process involves committees (standing, select, conference), floor debate, and reconciliation — the Senate's filibuster requires 60 votes for cloture to end debate.
Congressional behavior is shaped by the trustee model (own judgment), delegate model (constituent wishes), and politico model (a blend), plus logrolling, pork barrel, and party pressure.
Presidential power includes formal powers (veto, commander in chief, appointments, treaties) and informal powers (executive orders, executive agreements, bully pulpit, signing statements).

Judicial review (Marbury v. Madison, 1803) gives courts the power to strike down unconstitutional laws; judicial philosophy ranges from activism (broad interpretation) to restraint (deference to legislature).
The bureaucracy implements laws through discretionary authority and rule-making; it is held accountable by Congress (oversight, appropriations), the president (appointments), and the courts.
Iron triangles link congressional committees, interest groups, and bureaucratic agencies in stable policy alliances; issue networks are broader, more fluid coalitions of diverse stakeholders.
Presidential power has expanded through executive orders, executive agreements, and the use of emergency powers — checked by the War Powers Resolution (1973) and congressional oversight.

key_terms.definition: A two-chamber legislature: the House of Representatives (proportional) and the Senate (equal representation).
The presiding officer of the House who controls the floor agenda and is second in presidential succession.
A Senate tactic of extended debate used to delay or block a vote on legislation.
A Senate procedure requiring 60 votes to end debate and bring a measure to a final vote.
A permanent congressional committee that handles legislation in a specific policy area (e.g., Judiciary, Armed Services).
A temporary joint committee formed to reconcile differences between House and Senate versions of a bill.
Manipulating district boundaries to advantage a party or group — includes 'packing' and 'cracking' strategies.
A presidential directive that manages federal operations with the force of law, without congressional approval.
A pact with a foreign leader that does not require Senate ratification, unlike a formal treaty.
The power of courts to declare laws and executive actions unconstitutional — established in Marbury v. Madison (1803).
Order by which the Supreme Court agrees to hear a case from a lower court; requires the Rule of Four.
The principle of following precedent — courts respect prior rulings to ensure legal consistency.
Judges interpret the Constitution broadly and may overturn precedent or strike down laws to protect rights.
Judges defer to the elected branches and interpret the Constitution narrowly, following original intent.
A stable, mutually beneficial alliance among a congressional committee, a bureaucratic agency, and an interest group.
The complex system of federal agencies and departments that implement and administer government policy.
The power of agencies to interpret and implement legislation through specific rules and regulations.
1973 law requiring the president to notify Congress within 48 hours of deploying troops and limiting deployment to 60 days.

exam_tip: FRQ questions often ask how one branch checks another — be ready to cite specific mechanisms (e.g., Senate filibuster, presidential veto override, judicial review) and connect them to constitutional principles.
